chapters.title: You cannot stop anxiety, but you can change how you meet it
chapters.text: Three false assumptions sit behind most anxious habits. The first is that you cannot stand a moment of uncertainty. The second is that you have to be perfect to count. The third is that you owe the world more than you owe yourself. None of those beliefs hold up under a closer look. You can thrive in situations that have no clear answer. You do not have to be perfect to be effective. And each person is responsible for their own health, well-being, and joy. When anxiety takes the wheel, most of us fall back on the same handful of moves. A person who worries about leaving the gas on may check the stove two or three times before they leave the house, and they may even walk back to the stove once they are already in the car. The author Jennifer Shannon calls those moves safety strategies. They are the small habits that soothe the worry in the short term, and they feed the worry in the long term. The next time you feel anxious, pay attention to the things you do to ease the feeling. That list is your safety strategy in plain sight. The first step to change is to see the pattern. Once you can name it, you can take it apart.

chapters.title: Listen for the chatter of the monkey
chapters.text:
A monkey in your head sounds like a strange image, yet it goes back thousands of years. Early thinkers compared the human brain to a restless monkey that leaps from one branch to the next without ever resting. Modern brain scans back the picture up. The anxious mind jumps between worries the way a monkey swings from tree to tree. The constant chatter of what-if thoughts is what therapists call the monkey mind. The monkey is greedy for food, and the food it craves is more worry. Every time you run a safety check, every time you ask a friend for reassurance, every time you scan the room for danger, you toss another handful into its mouth. The monkey never sleeps, and the harder you try to silence it, the louder it gets. The good news is that you can change your relationship with the monkey.

The key shift is this. Anxiety is not who you are. It is a small part of you, and you can step back from it. Add up the hours you spend worrying in a week. It is unlikely that worry fills all one hundred and sixty-eight hours. Worry is a slice of your life, not the whole pie. An estimated two hundred and seventy-five million people around the world live with an anxiety disorder. You are not alone, and the monkey does not have to drive the bus.

chapters.title: The amygdala scans for danger that is not there
chapters.text:
Anxiety keeps the body on a low boil of stress. The brain drips out stress hormones that keep the worry alive. The amygdala, a small almond-shaped organ near the top of the spine, runs the threat scan. Every sight, sound, and thought passes through it on the way to the rest of the brain. When the amygdala senses danger, it sets off the alarm and floods the body with fight-or-flight chemicals. The system worked well on the savanna. It can misfire in the modern world, and the misfires are what we call anxiety. The amygdala often sounds the alarm for threats that are not real. Your brain is often wrong, and that is a hard fact for anxious people to swallow.

Once the alarm rings, the anxious mind makes two big mistakes. It overstates the size of the threat, and it understates the person's ability to handle it. From there, the safety strategies kick in, and the cycle spins faster. You press pause on your life. You scan every room for danger. You lose hours to checking, listing, and stalling. Knowing how the loop works is the first step toward breaking it. You can take the wheel back, and the chapters ahead will show you how.

chapters.title: Three false beliefs that keep the loop alive
chapters.text:
Most anxiety grows out of three beliefs that don't hold up. The first is that you cannot bear a moment of uncertainty. The second is that everything has to be perfect for you to feel safe. The third is that you have to carry the load for everyone around you. None of these is true. You can do well in a world that does not hand you clean answers. You do not have to nail every task to count as a success. And each person is in charge of their own well-being, not yours. The same safety strategies that show up again and again are the fuel the monkey eats. Checking the stove, double-checking the lock, rehearsing every conversation five times in your head. The very moves that you are doing to control your anxiety are feeding the monkey.

The cycle runs in a loop. You sense a threat, real or imagined. The thought sparks anxiety. Anxiety pushes you toward a safety strategy. The strategy gives you a short break, and the break confirms that the threat was real. The next time, the same loop fires a little faster. To break the cycle, you have to challenge the thought before you reach for the strategy. Ask what you are really afraid of, what the worst case would be, and what would happen if the worst case came true. Most of the time, the answer shows you that the threat was a paper tiger. Two kinds of strategies keep the loop alive. Behavioral strategies mean doing something, like checking the gas one more time. Mental strategies mean overthinking, researching the same topic for hours, or making a long list to keep a worry at bay. Both kinds drain energy and do not solve the problem. Write a list of your own safety strategies, and you will see the pattern in a new light. That list is the map you need to start changing the loop.

chapters.title: The four-step cycle of worry
chapters.text:
Safety strategies feel helpful in the moment, and that is the trap. The short relief they give you teaches the brain that the strategy was necessary. Over time, the strategy itself becomes proof that the threat was real. The cycle of anxiety has four steps. A threat appears, real or misread. Anxiety lights up. You run a safety strategy for relief. The relief confirms the danger. The more you run the cycle, the more deeply the threat gets wired into your brain. To break out, you have to challenge the perceived threat and then hold off on the safety move. Ask yourself what you are really afraid of, what the worst case would be, and what it would mean if the worst case came true. Most of the time, the answer is much smaller than the worry suggested. The two flavors of safety strategy are the do-something kind, like checking the gas, and the think-something kind, like over-researching a topic or making endless lists. Both add to the load and do not solve the original problem. Unhealthy coping does not help anxiety. It feeds it.

The author Jennifer Shannon suggests a simple exercise. Write a list of the safety strategies you lean on most. Then look at the list and ask how much of your day you are willing to swap for a few minutes of relief. Once you see the cost, the next step is to start cutting the habits that cost the most.

chapters.title: Trade the monkey mindset for an expansive one
chapters.text:
Once you can name your safety strategies, the next move is to challenge them one at a time. That is hard at first, yet it gets easier with practice. The trick is to swap a tight monkey mindset for a more open one. If you have to be sure of everything to feel safe, try a new line that says uncertainty is okay sometimes. If you have to nail every task to feel good, try a line that says mistakes are part of the deal. Jennifer Shannon calls this new outlook an expansive mindset. It changes how you act, and it opens the door to new experiences. The more you try, the more your brain starts to wire the new line into place.

Aim to develop an expansive mindset to challenge anxious thoughts and add a dose of positivity. The monkey mindset loses some of its grip when you stop feeding it. The expansive mindset does not erase anxiety, but it can hit the override button and give you more control. When the next wave of worry shows up, you can let it pass without obeying it. You are training the brain to file the moment in the not-threatening folder. All feelings, even the loud ones, have a beginning, a middle, and an end. Once you learn to let them run their course, they stop running your day.

chapters.title: Turn down the volume on the chatter
chapters.text:
Jennifer Shannon calls the running tape of worries the monkey chatter. The good news is that you can turn the volume down on that tape. The thoughts do not have to be obeyed, and they do not have to be fought. You can let them drift in and out of your ears like passing clouds. Shannon offers a six-step recipe for any worry that lands on you. Acknowledge the problem and name it. Brainstorm four actions you could take. Picture what each action would lead to. Pick the best action from the list. Try it for a stretch of time. Pat yourself on the back when it works. The chatter gets quieter as you act, and the monkey grows bored of the empty room. When you hear the next round of noise, just say thanks, monkey, and let it pass.

One of the loudest beliefs anxious people carry is that everything has to be sure to be safe. Life is rarely sure, and that is part of its beauty. Be flexible when something goes wrong, and be grateful when something goes right. Make a small plan to handle the bad day when it lands, and then move on. The flow of life often carries you somewhere you would never have planned, and that is a gift.

chapters.title: Conclusion: mute the monkey and take your life back
chapters.text: Anxiety is common, and it does not have to run your days. The habit of trying to control it often makes it louder. The way out is to change what you do, and the change starts with a list. Write down the safety strategies you lean on, and you will see how much space they take up in your day. Learn a simple breathing pattern, like four counts in, seven counts held, eight counts out, and run it three times when the noise rises. Challenge the threat in your head before you reach for the strategy. The brain is not always right, and most of the worst-case stories never come true. Give yourself credit for the work you have already done. You are stronger than the chatter gives you credit for, and the monkey is smaller than it looks. Anxiety is fear, and fear is a story the mind tells. Stories can be rewritten. When you hit the mute button on the monkey, you will sleep better, decide faster, and enjoy the day more. Start tonight. The work is small, and the reward is large.
